essay_text:
A toxic smell rushed through my nose as I groaned in pain. I rubbed my eyes to wake myself up and as soon as
they opened, all I could see was white smoke seeping through the hotel room. My teammates frantically searched
around to find the source of the smoke while I panicked due to the piercing sound of the fire alarm and
unbreathable air.
Since we didn’t know the origin of the smoke, we thought there was a huge fire in the hallway outside of our room.
Thus, we did not dare open the door and we kept our windows locked. We were trapped. As the smoke filled the
room, it irritated my nose and throat, making it unbearable to breathe.
Our boat was spending the night together because we had to prepare for the league championship race the day
before. As a freshman on the varsity rowing team, I didn’t know how to react to the emergency situation. I just sat
there on the bed, coughing in pain while the upperclassmen looked for solutions and made phone calls to the
lobby.
When the smoke was at its peak, one of the seniors took off his shirt and tossed it to me so I could use it to cover
my nose and mouth. Even though the air was poisonous for everyone, the seniors endured the pain to control the
situation and to reassure nervous teammates. After inspecting the room, the firefighters discovered that one of the
electrical cords had caught on fire. In that moment, I was relieved but at the same time, embarrassed.
Instead of helping to de-escalate the situation, I relied on others to do it for me. I realized that I liked to act tough in
front of others but when it came down to emergency situations, I shied away in fear.
On the other hand, the seniors that acted with bravery and initiative were calm and sincere in their daily lives.
Their true leadership came from displaying self-sacrifice and willingness to help others in moments of chaos.
Leadership and courage are not defined by boasting one’s masculinity but instead by showing up when others
need you.
The leadership and care I witnessed that night laid the foundation for me to give back as a leader of the crew
team. When the world came to an abrupt stop this spring, our team had to face an unprecedented obstacle. In an
instant, everyone went back to their homes and was under different circumstances. For a couple weeks, our team
was fairly inactive because the physical distance had a larger impact than we thought. The seniors had lost the
desire to carry the team once the news came down that our season was cancelled. As the younger guys started to
lose motivation, somebody had to step up and take the lead.
From South Korea, I contacted every member of the team to check in on how they were doing, made training
plans for each person’s situation, organized team meetings through Zoom, held virtual practices, and rallied the
other juniors to help me pick the team back up. As the team rekindled the fire we once had, the team started to
look at me as the new leader of the team. Rather than the usual passing of the torch that the coach announces, it
was a gradual change in leadership through action.
Unlike the hotel room incident where we were all in the same space, I had to take a different approach to
leadership in times of great difficulty and distance. Whatever form it takes, true leadership comes from doing what
no one else is willing to do when faced with challenges.
prompt: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts narratives beginning with a vivid, visceral incident to establish an initial emotional state, then pivots to deep self-reflection on personal shortcomings. You meticulously detail your journey of growth, using a subsequent challenge to demonstrate evolved leadership through concrete actions. Employ clear, direct language, balancing descriptive imagery with insightful analysis of values like courage and responsibility.
sentence_style: Predominantly clear and declarative sentences, with moderate length variation. Descriptions often use sensory details to create immediacy, while reflective sections maintain a steady, analytical rhythm. There are no fragments, and the structure is generally formal but accessible.
vocabulary_level: Moderate — The language is precise and descriptive without being overly complex or academic, prioritizing clarity and directness.
structural_patterns: The essay follows a 'contrast and growth' narrative arc. It begins with an anecdotal emergency, shifts to a moment of self-realization and explicit lesson-stating, then provides a second, contrasting anecdote to demonstrate the application of that lesson and personal evolution.
distinctive_moves: Opening with a high-stakes, sensory-rich incident.
Explicitly stating a personal flaw or realization directly after an anecdote.
Using direct contrast to highlight growth (past self vs. present self, past situation vs. present situation).
Employing a list of specific actions to illustrate leadership and effort.
Concluding with a universal lesson or definition derived from personal experience.
themes_and_preoccupations: The true nature of leadership and courage.
Responsibility and initiative in challenging circumstances.
The transition from passive observation to active participation.
self_presentation: The writer initially presents as vulnerable and self-critical, admitting fear and embarrassment. They then shift to a self-aware and reflective stance, ultimately presenting as proactive, mature, and a demonstrated leader.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: The specific context of a varsity rowing team provides a backdrop for teamwork and hierarchical dynamics. The mention of 'From South Korea' adds a layer of global context and highlights the challenge of remote leadership and overcoming distance.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly abstract or theoretical language without concrete examples.
A lack of explicit self-reflection or admission of past shortcomings.
Skipping the clear 'cause and effect' of how a lesson was learned and applied.
sample_sentence: A similar moment of clarity struck me when our debate team, facing an unexpected forfeit, realized true collaboration meant every voice had to build, not just argue, if we were to turn failure into a shared victory.
